pages_preview.text_preview: 1 Assessments of Credibility in the Social and Behavioral Sciences Anna Lou Abatayo, Titipat Achakulvisut, Daniel Acuna, Balazs Aczel, Laxmaan Balaji, Anita Bandrowski, Daniel M. Benjamin, Michael Bishop, Gary L. Brase, Andrew Brown, Martin Bush, James Caverlee, Tatiana Chakravorti, Yiling Chen, Mason Daley, Morteza Dehghani, Mirka Dirzo, Anna Dreber, Peter Eckmann, Timothy M. Errington, Qizhang Feng, Fiona Fidler, Samuel Field, Nicholas Fox, Robert Fraleigh, Aaron B. Frank, Hannah Fraser, James Gentile, C Lee Giles, Brandon Goldfedder, Phil Gooch, Michael Gordon, Elliot Gould, Christopher Griffin, Timothy R. Gulden, Noah Haber, Krystal M. Nosek*, Michèle B. Nuijten, David Pennock, Thomas Pfeiffer, Darien Pipkin, Jay Pujara, Sarah Rajtmajer, Martijn Roelandse, Adam Russell, Priya Silverstein, Vaibhav Singh, Courtney K. Soderberg, Anna Squicciarini, Theresa Stankov, Jordan W. Suchow, Barnabas Szaszi, Louisa Tran, Charles R Twardy, Andrew H. Tyner, Peter Vesk, Timothy Vines, Colby J. Vorland, Juntao Wang, Zhuoer Wang, Xin Wei, David P. Wilkinson, Bonnie C. Wintle, and Jian Wu *correspondence: nosek@cos.io Abstract Credibility assessment — determining whether research findings are trustworthy or believable — is essential to the research process. One aspect of credibility is repeatability, which includes assessing whether consistent results are obtained when using new data to answer the same question (replicability), when repeating the original analyses with the original data (reproducibility), or when conducting alternative analyses about the same question with the original data (robustness). These features of repeatability differ in the resources required to investigate them, and it is unknown how they relate with one another and with other features of credibility. We investigated relationships among credibility measures in a stratified random sample of claims made across the social and behavioral sciences. Measures of repeatability were modestly correlated with each other (r’s = 0.30, -0.04, -0.23) though the correlation between robustness and reproducibility is likely misestimated because selecting claims for robustness testing was partly contingent on reproducibility success. Replicability and human and machine predictions of replicability were modestly correlated (Median r = 0.23; Range = - 0.10 to 0.47). Though estimated with substantial uncertainty in some cases, no discipline showed consistently higher repeatability than other disciplines across measures. For example, Education had the highest replicability estimate (0.63, 95% CI [.32 - .86]) and the lowest reproducibility estimate (0.25, 95% CI [.25, 95% CI [.06 - .38]), whereas Economics had the lowest replicability estimate (0.43, 95% CI [.28 - .64]) and nearly the highest reproducibility estimate (0.70, 95% CI [.56 - .85]). Repeatability measures were modestly and heterogeneously associated with other potential indicators of credibility (Median |r| = 0.08; Range = 0.01 to 0.55). Credibility assessment is multidimensional with substantial opportunity for innovation and validation of its measurement. Keywords: replication, reproducibility, robustness, credibility, validity, machine learning, social and behavioral sciences

2 Science is a continuous dialogue between the generation of evidence and evaluation of explanations. This dialogue occurs within researchers as they pursue discoveries and between researchers through discussion and debate. Credibility assessment is necessary for science to be self-correcting, as it helps root out flaws and identify gaps between evidence and explanation. Progress is made by improving explanations to account for present evidence and to predict future evidence. Recognizing that popularized indicators such as journal impact factor and institutional prestige have weak validity as indicators of credibility,1–5 there is burgeoning interest in developing and validating credibility measures for research findings, researchers, and research supporting institutions.6–8 One aspect of credibility, replicability, refers to investigating the same question with independent data to determine if the findings are repeatable.9–14 Successful independent replications indicate the discovery of a regularity in nature and, perhaps, understanding of the conditions sufficient to observe that regularity. This adds credibility to the finding, which can be advanced further by gathering converging evidence for the explanation and ruling out alternatives. However, conducting replications is burdensome and slow. Less resource-intensive human14–19 or machine20–23 methods might be capable of predicting replication outcomes, providing easy and rapid credibility signals that support allocating resources for more intensive verification efforts. Replicability is part of a broader concept of repeatability that includes reproducibility and robustness testing.9,24 Reproduction refers to investigating the same question as a prior study with the same data using the same analysis.25–31 Robustness testing refers to investigating the same question with the same data using justifiable alternative analyses.32–35 A presumption of all three of these methods is that credibility is enhanced by showing that answers to scientific questions are unchanged when different steps of the research process are repeated. However, there is little evidence about how these repeatability measures are related to each other and how they vary across research fields. Repeatability is one part of the even broader concept of credibility.6 For example, credibility is advanced by addressing potential competing interests in the conducting and reporting of research, by taking existing knowledge into account, by using methods that have been validated, by controlling for biases in the research context, by pursuing precise and reliable evidence, and by calibrating the strength of the claims to the uncertainty of the evidence. Several measures have been offered, though not necessarily validated, as indicators of credibility such as: evidence of intellectual humility by researchers;36 automated methods for detecting reporting errors, data sharing, adherence to reporting standards, and disclosure of competing interests;37–42 citation rates;43 and even stereotypes such as presuming that wealthier nations produce more reliable research. Despite their centrality to research evaluation, there is little empirical evidence about the measurement or relationships among different aspects of credibility. In this paper, we explore relationships among repeatability and credibility measures for a sample of published social and behavioral science findings, produced in the DARPA-funded

3 Systemizing Confidence in Open Research and Evidence (SCORE) program. The primary purpose of the SCORE program was to develop and test explainable algorithms for predicting replicability. The program’s design served this purpose first, which is reported in Rajtmajer et al,44 and provided a unique opportunity for exploring credibility measures second, which is reported here. SCORE sampled claims from 3900 papers published from 2009 to 2018 in 62 journals across the social and behavioral sciences. For SCORE, a claim is a reported finding associated with a statistical inference. Claims were subjected to credibility assessment with a variety of methods including: three independent teams that developed algorithms to predict the replicability of claims;44 two independent teams that used human assessment methods to predict the replicability of claims;45 dozens of teams assessing the reproducibility,46 robustness,34 and replicability47 of evidence supporting the claims; and several measures of the original research and reporting that could be indicators or correlates of credibility. The cited sources provide in depth reporting about each credibility measure independently. Here, we use this unique and expansive dataset to explore [1] relations among human, machine, and empirical assessments of replicability, [2] relations among empirical measures of repeatability, and [3] relations between measures of repeatability and other potential indicators of research credibility. The main text reports outcomes by paper, weighting for cases with multiple claims per paper. Tables S18-S21 and Figures S13-S14 report the same results from the main text by claims instead of by papers. We observe mostly modest relations between the credibility measures. This highlights the heterogeneity of credibility assessment and the opportunity for innovation in the conceptualization and measurement of research credibility. Results Relations among replicability predictions and outcomes We first examined predictions of replicability rates by two human methods and four machine methods, compared to observed replicability rates for a sample of claims from the social and behavioral sciences. Several independent teams developed methods to predict replication success of individual research findings using human judgments or machine algorithms. These are compared with the outcomes of replication studies conducted on a portion of the assessed findings. To reduce false positive risk in exploration, we conducted a principal component analysis for each human and machine team on scores from several variants of their methods that each team used during the program of research. This has the benefit of providing a single value summarizing each method, and the cost of ignoring unique signals in individual variants (Figure S10). The results are a conservative indicator of performance relative to the best- performing variants. Evidence about performance across variants of machine and human methods can be found in supporting information and other reports.45 One algorithm team (Synthetic Markets) had a primary team and a red team, so we separated those into A and B teams before conducting the principal component analysis.

4 Table 1. Descriptive statistics for replicability measures All available data Sample in common with replication outcomes N Median Mean SD N Median Mean SD Humans Structured 3900 .61 .59 .16 108.9 .57 .56 .19 elicitations Prediction 3000 .50 .55 .23 133 .52 .56 .24 markets Machines A+ 1579 .60 .57 .21 49.1 .50 .49 .27 MACROSCORE 1579 .60 .58 .18 49.1 .55 .55 .21 Synthetic markets 1499 .62 .57 .21 52 .59 .57 .24 A Synthetic markets 1499 .57 .59 .17 52 .55 .56 .16 B Empirical Replicability 164 -- .49 -- 164 -- .49 -- Caption: SD = standard deviation, N = sample size; The prediction target for the methods was a dichotomous outcome of whether a replication attempt would achieve a statistically significant result with the same pattern as the original study. All methods are scaled from 0 (replication failure) to 1 (replication success). Replicability was dichotomous, so no median or SD is represented. Table 1 presents descriptive statistics by paper for assessments of replicability, and Figure 1 presents each method’s distribution and correlation with other methods. All methods used a 0 (not replicable) to 1 (replicable) scale, with values interpretable as the probability of replicating successfully. Fractions of papers can occur because of weighting when multiple claims were assessed in the same paper. Mean and median estimates across methods ranged from 0.50 to 0.62. The mean and median estimates clustered above the observed replicability rate of 0.49. Predictions were made for a large sample of claims, and only a subset were subjected to a replication attempt. The right half of Table 2 shows the estimates for only those claims with replications attempted. They ranged from 0.49 to 0.59, similar to the full sample, with the exception of A+ being smaller and precisely consistent with the observed replicability rate. Considering central tendencies, human and machine assessments were relatively consistent with observed replicability rates.

5 Figure 1. Distributions and correlations among replicability measures Caption: Elicitations and markets are human assessments; A+, MACROSCORE [MACRO], Synthetic Markets A [Synthetic A], and Synthetic Markets B [Synthetic B] are machine assessments. Replicability is the outcome of a replication attempt defined as statistical significance (p < .05) with the same pattern as the original finding. The upper triangle shows correlation coefficients with their 95% confidence intervals. We next examined correlations among human, machine, and actual replicability assessments. Human assessments were positively correlated with each other (0.47), but not redundant (Figure 1). Machine assessments were weakly to moderately interrelated (Median = 0.28; Range = -0.08 to 0.39) with Synthetic Markets A being most distinct. Machine assessments were weakly to moderately positively correlated with human assessments (Median = 0.28; Range = 0.02 to 0.35). Human and machine assessments were modestly positively correlated with dichotomous replication outcomes (Median = 0.16; Range = -0.10 to 0.26). Note that the program requirements were for machine assessments to predict human assessments.44 The descriptive statistics and correlation matrix are summary estimates of available data, and do not remove poor-performing variants or attempt to account for improvement in methods over time as the program included iterative performance cycles. The supporting information presents the best performing variants (Tables S22 and S23; Figure S13). To summarize those findings: human assessments were strongly correlated (r = 0.50), machine assessments were weakly to moderately interrelated (Median = 0.25; Range -0.01 to 0.51) and weakly to moderately correlated with human assessments (Median = 0.28; Range 0.09 to 0.51). Best-performing human and machine assessments were weakly to moderately correlated with replication outcomes (Median = 0.15; Range 0.02 to 0.30).

6 Figure 2. Correlations among measures of repeatability Caption: Replicability (same question, new data), robustness (same question, same data, new analyses), and reproducibility (same question, same data, same analysis) assess the repeatability of evidence for a prior claim in different ways. Outcomes are scaled from 0 (failure) to 1 (success). Replicability is dichotomous, reproducibility has three levels (0.5 indicated approximate success), and robustness is relatively continuous. The upper triangle shows correlation coefficients with their 95% confidence intervals. Follow-on research could explore potential moderators of these relationships. For example, human and machine assessments appeared to be somewhat more strongly correlated with replication outcomes for fields with methodologies similar to the original replication outcome training data (psychology and marketing; Median = 0.18; Range = -0.13 to 0.36) than for other fields (Median = 0.10; Range = -0.34 to 0.19). Overall, however, the relationships were relatively modest. Relations among repeatability measures In addition to replication attempts, we conducted robustness tests and reproductions of original claims. Reproducibility involved conducting the same analysis on the same data and observing whether the same result was observed as reported originally with three levels: Not reproduced, approximately reproduced, and precisely reproduced.46 Robustness involved at least five independent analysts each testing the same question with the same data with the analysis strategy that they believe was most justified, the outcome being the proportion of analysts’
